question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a network partition that makes all Execute() calls fail, so that submitWithRetry() returns 'all attempts failed: %w' wrapping only the last error, and since the error is only logged (not alerted), the pinger silently continues to the next tick—creating a monitoring blind spot where the network is partitioned but no automated alert is triggered?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that hiero.NewTransferTransaction() is called fresh on every retry attempt, meaning the transaction's valid start time is reset on each attempt, so during a partition where the first attempt's transaction was committed but the response was lost, the retry submits a transaction with a different valid start time—making it impossible to detect the duplicate via transaction ID matching?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a network partition that makes the pinger's retry loop consume all available file descriptors (each Execute() attempt opens a new gRPC connection that is not closed on error), eventually causing the process to fail with 'too many open files' and crash, triggering a container restart that resets the readiness probe?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: backoff()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that backoff() is a pure function with no jitter, so that if multiple pinger instances are deployed and all experience the same network partition simultaneously, they will all retry at exactly the same intervals—creating synchronized retry storms that hit the consensus nodes in coordinated bursts upon partition recovery?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a network partition that makes Execute() fail on every attempt, and since the pinger does not implement any backpressure or rate limiting beyond the fixed backoff, the total number of failed transactions submitted to the network per hour is (3600 / cfg.interval.Seconds()) * (cfg.maxRetries + 1), which at default settings (1s interval, 10 retries) is 39,600 failed transaction attempts per hour?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the pinger does not distinguish between a network partition error and a permanent configuration error (e.g., invalid account ID) in the retry logic, so that a partition-induced error that corrupts the toID resolution would cause the pinger to retry indefinitely against a non-existent account, wasting resources?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a network partition that makes the Hiero SDK switch to a backup consensus node mid-transaction, causing the TransferTransaction to be submitted to a different node than intended, and if that node is also partitioned, the transaction is silently dropped without the pinger detecting the failure until the receipt poll times out?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the pinger's retry loop does not implement a maximum total duration (only a maximum number of attempts), so that if the SDK's per-call timeout is very long (e.g., 60 seconds) and cfg.maxRetries=10, a single submitWithRetry() call can block for over 10 minutes during a partition, completely stalling the pinger's monitoring function?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a network partition that makes GetReceipt() return a non-nil error after Execute() succeeds, causing the pinger to set err = rerr and proceed to retry—but since the transaction was already committed, the retry submits a new transaction, and the pinger's success metric is only incremented when both Execute() and GetReceipt() succeed, creating a false negative in the monitoring data?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the pinger does not validate the receipt status (receipt.Status.String() is only logged, not checked for error codes), so that during a partition-induced partial failure, a transaction could receive a non-SUCCESS status (e.g., BUSY, PLATFORM_NOT_ACTIVE) but the pinger would still return nil and log it as a success?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a network partition that makes the Hiero SDK's internal retry mechanism (separate from submitWithRetry's own retry loop) also retry, causing a multiplicative explosion of actual transaction submissions: (SDK internal retries) * (cfg.maxRetries + 1) attempts per tick, far exceeding the operator's intended retry budget?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the pinger does not implement any state persistence, so that after a partition-induced crash and restart, the pinger has no memory of in-flight transactions from before the crash—meaning transactions that were committed but whose receipts were never retrieved are permanently lost from the monitoring record?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a network partition that makes the pinger's goroutine block inside Execute() while the main.go ticker fires repeatedly, and since Go's time.Ticker channel has a buffer of only 1, all ticks beyond the first buffered one are dropped—causing the pinger to miss an unbounded number of monitoring intervals during a sustained partition without any indication of how many were missed?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the pinger's backoff timer (time.NewTimer(sleep)) is created inside the retry loop and not reused, so that during a partition with many retries, a large number of timer objects are created and garbage collected—causing GC pressure that further degrades the pinger's performance during an already-stressed partition scenario?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a network partition that makes Execute() return an error containing the operator's account ID or transaction details in the error message, which is then logged via log.Printf('attempt %d/%d failed: %v')—leaking sensitive operator account information to anyone with access to the container logs?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the pinger does not implement any admission control or rate limiting on the number of concurrent submitWithRetry() goroutines, so that if the ticker fires while a previous submitWithRetry() is still blocked during a partition, multiple goroutines can be executing submitWithRetry() simultaneously—each independently retrying and submitting transactions?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a network partition that makes the Hiero SDK's node health check mechanism mark all nodes as unhealthy, causing Execute() to fail immediately without even attempting a network call—but since the pinger's retry loop still waits for backoff between attempts, the pinger wastes time waiting for backoffs even though no network calls are being made?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the pinger does not check whether cfg.amountTinybar is negative before constructing the TransferTransaction, so that during a partition-induced configuration reload (if any), a negative amount could cause AddHbarTransfer() to add a positive transfer to the operator account—effectively reversing the intended transfer direction?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a network partition that makes the pinger's retry loop run to completion (all attempts exhausted) and return an error, but since main.go only logs the error and continues to the next tick, the pinger never enters a failed/degraded state—meaning the liveness probe (/tmp/alive) continues to be updated even though the pinger has been unable to submit any transactions for an extended period?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the pinger's context (ctx) is derived from signal.NotifyContext in main.go and is shared across all ticker ticks, so that if a partition causes a goroutine to call ctx.Err() and find it cancelled (due to an unrelated SIGTERM), the goroutine returns ctx.Err() immediately—but if the SIGTERM was caused by the liveness probe failing due to the partition, this creates a feedback loop where partition causes liveness failure causes shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a network partition that makes Execute() succeed but GetReceipt() return a RECEIPT_NOT_FOUND error (because the receipt hasn't propagated to the mirror node yet), causing the pinger to treat a successful transaction as failed and retry—submitting a duplicate transaction that the network processes as a separate valid transfer?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: backoff()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that backoff() uses integer bit-shifting (1<<(attempt-1)) which in Go produces an untyped integer that is then cast to time.Duration, and if attempt reaches 64 or higher (possible if cfg.maxRetries is set very high via HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_MAX_RETRIES env var), the shift result is 0, causing backoff() to return 0 duration and the retry to fire immediately?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a network partition that makes the pinger's Execute() calls fail with a gRPC UNAVAILABLE status, which the Hiero SDK may interpret as a retryable error and internally retry before returning to submitWithRetry()—causing the actual number of network calls to be (SDK internal retries per attempt) * (cfg.maxRetries + 1), potentially thousands of calls per tick?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the pinger does not implement any transaction ID tracking across retries, so that during a partition where some Execute() calls succeed and some fail, the pinger has no way to determine which transactions were actually committed—making it impossible to accurately report the true success rate of transfers during the partition?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a network partition that makes the pinger's goroutine block inside GetReceipt() for an extended period, and since the heartbeat goroutine in main.go writes to /tmp/alive every 15 seconds independently, the liveness probe continues to pass even though the pinger is functionally frozen—masking the partition from the container orchestration layer?
